sentences.text: Right.
Well, of course, everyone was full of anticipation that, finally, the moment is coming.
That it may be the end of the war.
I remember that many people were really afraid of the end of the war.
They didn't know who survived.
Whether they were left alone, what would they do with themselves?
This was a term called [GERMAN], like danger of-- of peace.
They didn't know what will-- they were really petrified of the end of the war.
This way, there was a group of people.
Every-- the work was being assigned to them.
They knew-- they do-- they did teamwork with everybody else.
But they were afraid.
They-- they didn't know who-- who was left alive and who perished.
Well, of course, with me, again, it was a little bit different.
I was my mother and I knew that-- I couldn't wait to be reunited with my-- my father and my brother.
No.
No, there were many who were-- there were single, young boys and girls.
And they were all alone, not knowing what's waiting for them once the war is over-- who survived and-- and who didn't.
And there were a few who were with-- that was the beauty of being with Schindler, that there were families.
There were people with-- with parents, with brothers, with sisters.
Women--
Women lived separately and men lived separately.
Well, not-- not in Brunnlitz.
They were-- there-- it started with future, but then they were sent to-- to Auschwitz.
And subsequently, they survived.
But they-- there were no-- I don't recall little children in Brunnlitz, no.
I'm trying to think.
The picture is not-- not very clear at this moment.
While we were there?
Oh, of course.
I mean, this was living a communal life.
It's-- we did everything together.
And we worked together.
We-- we even sang songs together in the evening to-- to keep our spirit up a little bit.
And--
Well, I tell you, we were liberated by-- by the Russians.
And that was a sad liberation.
We were very much afraid of the Russians.
We-- we heard what they are doing to women.
And we-- we really were hoping for Americans to liberate us.
And-- so-- but nevertheless, we're at the end of the war and everybody was thinking in terms what-- what-- what to do next.
We knew that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually, we-- we-- after the war end-- after we were liberated, we worked our way to-- to Prague in Czechoslovakia.
We stayed there for several weeks.
Oh, by the way, I should mention that before we-- we were dismissed from-- because we were allowed to leave, Schindler gave every former inmate of camp a-- a ration of-- of fabrics and threads.
I don't know how he got hold of it.
This was the place where the ammunition factory was.
It was once a knitwear factory.
And there were some kinds of fabrics and-- and yarn.
And he distributed among all the inmates so we had something to start our after-war life with.
To sew something for our-- some decent clothing, and so on.
Because we were all the time with-- in our stripped outfits.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So my mother and I, we-- we went to Prague.
Also, my aunt and my cousins.
And then, we decided that we will try to get to Holland to Rotterdam.
My father had business acquaintances and connections in Rotterdam.
And we felt that they will be able to help us.
And sure enough, we traveled.
By-- at that time, one didn't need a ticket.
You just jumped from train to it.
First, we spent in Prague about three or four weeks running from one kitchen soup to another kitchen soup.
And then we managed to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to travel to-- to Holland, to Rotterdam.
And we contacted this gentlemen, my-- my father's business acquaintance.
And he helped us a little bit, but not too much.
Basically, we-- we contacted the organization for the refugees, and we were placed in a home for repatriates in-- in Rotterdam.
And that's where we stayed for almost a year, nine months or so, waiting for the papers to come from the States.
My father and brother were already in-- in United States.
And we as a first and second preference quota.
My-- my mother as a wife and I as a daughter.
We received our visa and passports.
And--
I want to go back some.
OK.
Yes.
There was no home.
We knew-- Krakow was not a home anymore.
We-- we have no place, really, to go back to.
And we-- we knew that our place is in the United States where my father is.
Yes, we knew that-- yes, that--
I am trying to think.
Probably, when we were in [?

Wolbrum, ?] some kind of-- there was some kind of a correspondence that they-- they left for-- I'm trying to think whether they were in Africa or they were-- no, we had a-- we had news that I had an uncle living in-- in the United States.
Or maybe even the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they knew-- maybe, even this gentleman in Rotterdam was the contact.
And through him, we knew exactly where my father is.
My father-- this was a different story.
It never entered his mind that we may not survive.
He never imagined.
He never conceived what's going on here.
So he lived with a-- he was a very successful businessman in Poland.
But when he came to the United States, he never thought of starting his own business.
He-- he thought in terms the war will be over and we'll go back-- all go back home to Krakow.
Never entered his mind that there is a need to start a new life.
You mentioned there was some fear of the liberation by the Russians.
Was that ever justified?
Well, they-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remembered when they came to-- in Brunnlitz to our camp, they, if I can say, kidnapped about three or four girls for-- for several days.
And we didn't know what happened to them, but the end result was that they came back.
And you have never seen happier four girls [LAUGHS] than the four of them, that's all I remember.
But we were afraid of them.
We really were afraid of them.
That they were violent.
That--
We just wanted to be liberated by-- liberated by the Americans.
That's--
Well, he was escorted by a group of Jews to make sure that nothing will happen to him.
Out of-- out of the camp until-- I don't remember where he-- he traveled to-- he went to Germany, I-- I think.
Well, my husband will tell you exactly.
Yeah.
Well, he-- he knew how we-- how we felt about him.
Have you seen the movie And you remember the incident when he's presented with that gold ring, which was made out of one of the man's gold teeth.
And this was the-- the gesture that-- you know, how much we appreciated.
There was not much we can-- we could give him.
But--
And after the war, we all stayed in touch with him.
And there was-- I don't think there was enough done for Oskar Schindler, because can you ever repay for-- for your life?
There is no price that you can pay for-- to someone for saving your life.
But there was an eternal gratitude.
And lately, it was Mrs.
Schindler.
There were many situations where she's being honored.
And--
I personally didn't.
I-- I-- I didn't-- there was just no-- no need.
I was not terribly sick that I would need, especially her favor.
But I-- I know of people.
My husband will tell you how-- how-- how much she did for-- for him and his family.
Well, we knew there is no point going back to Krakow.
And Prague was a large city.
And we-- we thought, we're from there.
We'll try to get in touch with-- with our family in the States.
Any-- any means of locomotion that was possible.
It could be train.
It could be a horse and buggy.
Whatever we could get on, we were traveling.
But mostly, we didn't need tickets, so we were just hopping from train to a train, asking about the directions.
And that's how we worked our way to.
With my mother, with my aunt, and my cousin.
For several weeks.
I don't remember whether four weeks or so.
Or, five weeks.
And then, we-- again, the same way we worked on way to Rotterdam, to Holland.
And I must say, the Dutch people were fantastic.
They were-- I mean, they-- We didn't know Dutch.
The only way we could communicate at that time was German.
And they-- they didn't trust anyone who spoke German at that time.
But nevertheless, we-- we managed to convince them and-- with the help of this-- my father's business friend who we are that we are inmates of-- previous inmates of concentration camp.
And they were fantastic, really.
They placed us in homes for repatriates and-- and fed us.
And that's where we waited for our papers to come to the United States.
Right after the war, the only person we spoke to was my father.
We-- for-- for weeks, we kept telling him.
And he just listened to us with wide open eyes.
Couldn't understand, couldn't grasp.
For instance, he made-- first he said, if you were not my wife and my daughter, I would never believe it.
Then he said, I don't understand.
If my family's hungry, I would go out and get them bread.
And it just-- it was impossible to-- to-- to describe it.
Not-- not-- not bad, yes, yes.
Yes.
I had a nervous stomach, but otherwise I was all right.
And so was my mother.
And we-- when we were in Rotterdam, I remember my brother was at that time-- he was with the British Royal Air Force, Polish division.
He was a bombardier.
He volunteered during the war.
And he came to visit us in Rotterdam.
And of course, it was an extremely emotional reunion.
And-- and then, after we got our papers, we went to United States.
And--
Oh, yes.
We-- for a while, we were toying with the idea, maybe with bringing her over here.
But at that point, she was already an old lady.
And to me, she always appeared to be old.
[LAUGHS] She was probably younger than I am today.
And we felt that she would be completely lost here without her friends and her-- and knowledge of language.
And she-- she had a life of her own in-- in Krakow.
But we kept sending her packages.
And we-- and we saw her once after.
When we had our papers to go to the United States, our passport and visa in Rotterdam, we-- we flew to Krakow to her.
She returned all my mother's jewelry and whatever she-- she kept for sentimental value.
And that was the-- the last time I have seen her.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
I personally did not, but I know of people that-- there were people who were hidden by Gentiles.
There were people who had valuables stored with Gentiles.
Oh, yes.
We were corresponding all the time.
As a matter of fact, when we were married here, she sent us a picture.
She made a party with her own friends.
And it was a toast, toasting to our long life and happiness, and-- and so on.
It was December 1946.
When got-- we came on a cargo.
I can call it a ship.
It was a boat had only six passengers on it.
And the trip was in December.
And the trip lasted 21 days.
And out of the 21 days, I-- we were seasick maybe for 19 or 20.
But once we arrived, and we-- we disembarked, there was a gentleman coming across from us.
And my mother looks at him and at me.
And she says, you know, this is-- this is Daddy.
And somehow, I-- it-- I-- I recognized him, but it was such a strange feeling after all those years to-- to see him.
And of course, he took off from work at the time.
After-- and he-- he was just going with us and buying us things, not that he was so well to do here.
After being such a successful businessman in Europe, he was working here as a-- a cafeteria cashier on  the night shift.
So his means were limited.
But nevertheless, he took a few weeks off.
And he saved up some money.
And he was going with us from department stores to department stores and buying things.
We were just bewildered with-- with all the goodies that were here.
I visualized life in America, you know, in-- in-- in comfort and great luxury.
I guess, to me, America and Hollywood was all the same.
[LAUGHS]
We lived in uptown Manhattan in New York on Audobon Avenue.
It was a small apartment, but everything was-- we were thrilled with everything.
It didn't really-- it was a modest, small apartment.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was in 1946.
And of course, we-- we came-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A few-- two weeks after we came, I started working.
I just-- I knew that everybody in America has to work.
And besides, being through-- going through concentration camps, I was not afraid.
And I would do just almost anything.
And while I lived in-- in Rotterdam, I was taking history of art.
And I did study some painting.
When I came to the States, I had to-- I felt I have to earn a living, even though my father was against it.
And I was painting on silk, fabrics, wood, metal, all kind of novelties.
And that's how-- I was thrilled with my first earned money.
I didn't know-- First of all, I bought gifts for my parents, and then I-- my father couldn't get over it.
He used to say, don't worry.
You have always time to spend it.
You just-- [LAUGHS]
They also came to-- their-- this aunt's husband also left Poland together with my father at the beginning of the war.
And they also went with us, the-- the aunt and cousin, to Rotterdam.
And the same-- the same way, there was a break of a few months.
They came earlier.
We came later.
Yeah.
Yes.
And then, one day I hear that my very dear, old friend, Ludwik Feigenbaum, is-- came from Germany.
He contacted me through my-- we didn't have a telephone.
He contacted my uncle.
And he in turn let me know that my present husband, Ludwik is at the HIAS.
And I got in touch with him.
I was overjoyed to find out that he is here.
We spent a few hours together.
He went to Chicago where his uncle made arrangement for them.
And we started corresponding.
And then, for a few years later, we became engaged and we married and then moved to New York.
Yeah, yeah.
Well, he was a jewelry salesman.
But I'd rather leave that to-- to-- to him.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, we have two sons.
One was born in 1951.
One was born in 1954.
Arthur, the older, and Lester, the younger.
And--
There was something, I-- oh, coming back.
After the war, before the family, when I-- when I started working here, painting all-- all novelties, it never occurred to me that I may take a day off, or a few-- a few days off without work.
So for instance, I would come-- I would start my work early in the morning in a painting workshop or studio in-- in Brooklyn.
And during lunch hour, I would buy a newspaper and-- and look at some ads and find that they are looking for hand painters, for artists, whatever, in-- in the Bronx.
Then, I would excuse myself under some pretext that I have to visit somebody.
I would go for an interview there and stay there already for the next job.
And come in-- I started in the morning in Bronx and come-- came back home from work from Brooklyn, let's say, and I was pretty-- pretty enterprising in those days.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then, of course, when-- when-- when we became engaged and then married, my-- my husband moved from Chicago to New York, together with his parents.
And we led a very close family life.
Like, for instance, every weekend was-- we knew we are spending it with our-- after we were married, with our parents.
Whether these were outings.
We bought our first cars, so we used to-- every Sunday, we used to take our parents for a-- a picnic or a-- a trip out-- out of town just to make their life a little bit more pleasant.
We-- for them, they had little difficulties with the new language.
By that time, we-- we could speak English.
And-- a then the children were born.
And there was a tremendous joys-- joy for us, and of course, for both sets of parents.
We were fortunate that we both had them.
And I feel-- I find myself extremely fortunate having survived with my mother and being reunited with my parents-- with my father and brother here in the States.
And as a result, maybe I-- I don't-- not that I don't remember, but certain-- certain facts are a little bit blurred.
I-- I just don't-- don't remember the chronological order.
And I had no-- no need to go back to all those happenings.
I just wanted to-- I didn't want to burden my children with my past.
I-- I felt-- maybe I didn't have enough sense of history in those days.
That-- that came later.
I felt they are American born and they should go on with-- with a normal life, whatever normal life is.
And--
We became quite friendly with many American couples.
And they asked, and we talked.
But once in a while, I realized that this is the-- just doesn't make sense to talk about it too much.
I felt the futility of it all.
Because they-- they cannot grasp it.
They-- you know, Elie Wiesel said it very well.
He said, how can you describe the indescribable?
And it-- it's-- it was very difficult to.
They would ask me questions that I was speechless.
How-- how can I explain to them what-- what the situations were?
What about most Americans, when you first came over--
Yes.
Yes.
If someone would ask me, how long are you here?
When did you come?
Well, they realized that we came from-- after being liberated from the camp.
So some felt sorry.
Some were in awe.
Some even didn't know how to talk to us.
You know, it's-- it's a very awkward situation.
Yes.
We have six granddaughters.
Each son has three girls.
And we are-- I am extremely [INAUDIBLE]-- I'm extremely proud of my two sons.
I better not start talking about it because I won't end.
[LAUGHS] And they have wonderful families.
They-- they are excellent family men, accomplished professional people.
And it's a good feeling to-- to have survived and to live to see that.
And--
Yes, there are situations.
There are moments that I-- that I recall certain incidents.
And--
For instance, friends still wonder why when I answer a telephone call, why I don't say hello as a matter or fact.
Only, it's always hello?
Like I'm wondering, who could it be?
And I still have that feeling in me.
It's not natural the telephone is ringing.
It's always wondering, who could it be?
It's-- and I-- I feel uncomfortable in large groups.
If there are gatherings, big gatherings I will go.
Especially now, I feel it's my obligation to attend all those.
But I don't feel very comfortable.
There is always-- that's when I go back to my wartime experiences.
Yes.
I don't think I am-- it-- it disturbs me in leading a normal life, but I am aware of it.
Like, I-- I don't, in particular, care-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't in particular for-- for women's luncheon, to be congregated together with women alone.
It always gets-- sends me back to-- brings me back to being together with a large group of women in a barrack.
You know I have certain-- I don't know, these are maybe not phobias, but there are certain feelings that are left over from those days.
Well, since I was so young when-- when it happened to me, I would say that in order to keep on going, hope is extremely-- extremely important.
That-- that-- that keeps you alive.
Maybe because I am basically-- I am a warrior, but I am basically a-- an optimist.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and, you know, like-- who-- who was it-- I think, if I may quote him-- John Milton who said that-- that a mind is place of its own.
It can make hell out of heaven and heaven out of hell.
And if you tried to-- to think about it, that doesn't mean that I don't have sleepless nights. but-- [LAUGHS]
Thank you.
